turns.content: I'm trying to flash my embedded firmware using cargo-embed but I keep getting errors when I run the build command. It seems like probe-rs is detecting a configuration issue in Embed.toml.

That's a common hurdle when transitioning to this toolchain. Before diving into the config, have you confirmed your target architecture matches what cargo-embed expects? Sometimes mismatched triplets or missing probe-probe identifiers cause silent failures that look like configuration errors but are actually environment mismatches.

Oh right, I assumed my current board is automatically compatible since it's an STM32. But maybe I need to explicitly set the chip type in Embed.toml under the [target] section? What exactly should go there?

You're partially correct but need to be specific about which probe-probe variant you're using. For STM32 chips, you typically specify something like 'probe-rs-stlink' or 'cargo-embed-stlink' depending on your exact hardware setup. If you're using a different debugger like OpenOCD, the identifier changes entirely and won't match what cargo-embed expects by default.

Got it, so I should update Embed.toml with the correct probe-probe identifier. But I'm still seeing an error about missing dependencies even after adding them to Cargo.toml. Is there something else cargo-embed needs beyond just the standard dev-dependencies?

You're right that cargo-embed has its own dependency graph separate from your crate's build-time deps. It requires probe-rs specific crates like probe-rs, probe-rs-probe, and sometimes embedded-hal traits for RTT logging to work properly. Also ensure you've enabled the flash-logging feature if you want those live debug messages during the burn process.
Wait, I thought cargo-embed automatically pulls in everything I need from my project's Cargo.toml? Can't it just resolve dependencies based on what my lib.rs uses?

That's a common misconception. cargo-embed doesn't auto-resolve your crate's internal dependencies for its own tooling; you must explicitly list probe-rs and related crates in Cargo.toml under the [build-dependencies] or [dev-dependencies] section depending on whether you're compiling during flash or just running the embed command. Without these, the build will fail even if your main code compines fine.

Ah okay so I need to add probe-rs and cargo-embed explicitly in Cargo.toml along with my existing dependencies. Does embedding RTT logging require any special configuration in Embed.toml besides just enabling the feature?

Yes, you'll want to set 'rtt' under [features] or enable it via the --features flag when invoking cargo-embed. Also make sure your target section specifies both the chip and probe-probe variant correctly so that the flasher knows exactly what firmware image to write and how to communicate with the debug hardware.

I'm trying to automate a firmware flash with cargo-embed, but I keep hitting an edge case where the RTT logging gets stuck in a loop even though my debug output looks fine. Specifically, I noticed that when I enable logging via `#[rprintln]`, the tooling hangs on the first iteration before it actually writes anything to the UART. It seems like something about how the probe-rs driver initializes the serial channel is interfering with the timing of the log calls.

That sounds like a classic initialization race condition in your test harness. The issue likely stems from how you're calling `rprintln!` before the probe-rs backend has fully bound to the UART device node. When cargo-embed runs, it invokes the embed script which sets up the RTT channel using the `probe-rs` crate's internal serialization logic. If your tooling tries to flush or log data immediately upon startup without waiting for the connection to be established by the probe-rs driver, the write buffer might get stuck because the underlying file descriptor hasn't been handed over to the kernel yet. You should probably wrap your logging calls in a small initialization block that explicitly waits for `Probe::connect()` to return before invoking any `rprintln!` macros.

Wait, I think I misunderstood what was happening. I assumed the hang was just because my code wasn't properly handling the `Error` trait from the probe-rs crate and didn't check if the connection succeeded before running the loop. But actually, the problem is that the tooling itself isn't waiting for the device to be ready; it's trying to write data to a stream that hasn't been opened yet by the embedded binary. So my assumption about `Result` handling was wrong because the issue is in the lifecycle of the connection, not the error propagation logic.

You're absolutely right on that distinction—the failure isn't about propagating errors from `probe-rs::Probe`, it's about the sequence of operations required to bring up the RTT channel. The tooling needs to ensure the `Embed` object in your binary has successfully executed the initialization script which binds the UART via probe-rs before attempting any `rprintln!` writes. If you move those logging calls into a function that runs only after confirming `probe-rs::Probe::connect()` returns `Ok`, the hang should disappear because the serial port will be fully handed over to the kernel context when your tooling finally tries to write the first log entry.

That makes sense, but I still don't see why my specific configuration in Embed.toml isn't triggering this. I had set `logging = true` and `rprintln!` macros enabled, which should be enough to activate RTT. Is there something about how cargo-embed parses the config that causes it to skip the initialization phase if certain flags are missing?

The configuration in Embed.toml is actually quite strict; simply setting `logging = true` isn't sufficient because cargo-embed requires explicit markers in your source code to enable RTT. You must ensure every function you want to log from includes a `#[rprintln]` macro call, and the tooling will only activate the probe-rs initialization if it detects these macros at compile time. If your config has those flags but the actual binary lacks the corresponding `impl` blocks or marker attributes, cargo-embed won't run the necessary init script to open the UART, leaving your tooling with a dangling reference until you add the missing macro invocations in your code.

I'm trying to flash my firmware using cargo-embed but I keep getting an error saying the chip is in a wrong state. I thought if I just set the correct boot mode in the configuration file, the probe-rs tool would automatically handle everything and never need me to manually reset the device or change any hardware settings.

That is a very common misunderstanding. While cargo-embed makes flashing much easier than raw probe-rs commands, it does not bypass the fundamental requirement of putting your microcontroller into the correct boot mode before you start writing code. Think of it like trying to pour water into a bucket that has been covered with a lid; no matter how perfect your measuring device or recipe is, if the container isn't open, nothing gets inside. The probe-rs tool can only write data when the chip's state allows it.

So I need to manually change something before running the command? Is there a specific flag or environment variable in cargo-embed that I should be using instead of relying on the Embed.toml configuration alone?

You don't necessarily need a special flag for every case; often, setting the boot mode correctly via your hardware or a simple reset cycle is enough. However, if you are using cargo-embed specifically to run code during debugging sessions, you might encounter issues where the tool cannot connect because it expects the chip to be in a specific state. The solution usually involves ensuring that your development board's jumpers are configured for the mode that cargo-embed needs, or occasionally resetting the device before starting the build process so the probe-rs driver can establish a connection. This prevents the 'wrong state' error you were seeing.

I'm trying to port my legacy C project to Rust, but I'm stuck on the boot loader issue. I wrote a simple Rust binary that prints "Hello" to stdout when run locally, and then I tried flashing it via probe-rs with cargo-embed using an empty Embed.toml file. It compiled fine, but after flashing, nothing happens on the board.

The problem is likely that your binary lacks a proper entry point for the flash memory region where the bootloader expects to find code. When you run cargo-embed without specifying where the image should go, it defaults to 0x0 which might not be valid on your chip's boot vector table. You need to define the memory layout in Embed.toml so that cargo-embed places the compiled object at the correct address for the CPU to fetch instructions from.
Ah okay, I see. But I thought since probe-rs handles flashing automatically, maybe it figures this out? Can't it just detect where my binary is and flash it directly?

That's a common misconception. While probe-rs manages the communication with the chip and handles erase/program cycles, cargo-embed controls exactly where in the memory map your Rust code lands. The flash memory isn't automatically aligned or initialized by the toolchain; you must explicitly tell cargo-embed which sections to include and at what addresses they should be placed. Without this configuration, the CPU reads garbage data from uninitialised regions instead of executing your startup code.
So I need to add sections in Embed.toml? What exactly do those look like?

Yes, you define memory regions with start addresses and sizes. For example, you might create a section called startup that spans from 0x08000 to 0x08100 where the reset handler lives, then another for your main binary starting at 0x08100. The format uses simple key-value pairs in Embed.toml like sections = [{ name = "startup", start = 0x8000, size = 0x100 }]. This tells cargo-embed to reserve those blocks and place your compiled .elf or .bin files accordingly.
Got it. But what about the reset vector? Does cargo-embed handle placing that automatically if I just specify my binary?

No, cargo-embed does not auto-generate or place the reset vector for you. You must ensure your Rust startup code (the entry point) is compiled into a specific section that maps to the correct address in memory. Typically this means writing a minimal C-compatible stub in Rust and compiling it as part of the binary so it appears at 0x08000, or using a separate assembly file if needed. probe-rs can flash whatever you give it, but it won't magically align your code to valid boot addresses without explicit instructions.
Okay so I need to write my own startup code in Rust? And then link it properly?

Exactly. You should create a small Rust function that acts as the reset handler and ensure it gets compiled into the correct section via Embed.toml or linker script if you're using custom configurations. Once cargo-embed builds with this, probe-rs will flash the complete image including your startup code at 0x08000 and your application after that. Then when you run probe-rs flash --chip <your-chip>, it will correctly initialize execution flow.
Wait, what about RTT logging? I want to add some debug output while the device is running from flash. Can cargo-embed do that?

Yes, cargo-embed supports RTT (Rust Targeted Runtime) logging through the embedded-rtt crate or similar tools. You configure this in your Cargo.toml dependencies and embed configuration to enable a log buffer on the flash memory. This allows you to send structured logs from your running firmware back to your host machine without needing to re-flash every time you change code, which is crucial for debugging during development cycles.
That sounds promising. But how do I actually use it during runtime? Do I need a special probe-rs command?

You don't need a special probe-rs command to enable RTT; you just compile the firmware with cargo-embed using the appropriate sections and flags. Once flashed, you can attach your debugger via probe-rs and send commands like 'probe-rs rtt read' or use the embedded RTT protocol directly from your host application to retrieve logs from the flash memory buffer. This lets you inspect runtime variables and log messages without needing to recompile or reboot.
Oh wow, that's really cool. So I can debug live code changes without flashing?

Not exactly full hot-swapping yet, but you can iterate quickly by re-flashing with cargo-embed while keeping the debugger attached. The key benefit is that once your startup and binary sections are configured correctly in Embed.toml, probe-rs handles the low-level flash operations cleanly without requiring manual memory alignment or vector table setup. This makes debugging embedded Rust projects much smoother than traditional bare-metal setups.

Perfect! So my next step is to define the startup section and main binary section in Embed.toml, then re-run cargo-embed build with probe-rs flash. And once that works, I can add RTT logging for future debugging sessions?

Exactly correct. Configure your memory layout first to get the basic boot sequence working, then layer on RTT capabilities as you need them. This approach gives you both reliable flashing and powerful runtime inspection tools without getting bogged down in low-level assembly details.
